# Consensus Guide: How to Write a Literature Review or Dissertation

![Literature Review & Dissertation How to Write Guide Literature Review & Dissertation How to Write Guide Type a useful name for the listAnd save to your new listClick ‘Create a new list’Click on the top right bookmark iconExporting references from Consensus to your reference softwareExporting references from Consensus to your reference softwareExporting references from Consensus to your reference softwareCheck references of key/recent papersSnowballing: check citations of older/high-impact papersSelect from high-quality journals onlySelect from high-quality journals onlyFind high-impact papers Find high-impact papers Analyzing a paperObtaining key summary dataBuilding your review (image 1)](https://framerusercontent.com/images/TqsSim4tFSEIqqe2nom9QXDqI.png?width=1456&height=816)

## Introduction

Writing a well-balanced, academically sound, and correctly referenced piece of work is essential for any student or academic. The ability to navigate and synthesize vast amounts of literature, while ensuring accuracy and relevance, is a critical skill. However, current tools like PubMed and Google Scholar, though useful, often fall short in maximizing the potential of the available research. Moreover, traditional AI tools such as ChatGPT may fabricate references, which undermines their value for serious academic work.

This is where Consensus steps in. By offering an academic-focused database and a multifunctional search bar, Consensus streamlines the research and writing process. Unlike other tools, Consensus is specifically designed to support rigorous academic work without compromising on the quality or integrity of references.

At Consensus, we are committed to upholding the highest academic standards. While the platform won’t write your Literature Review or Dissertation for you, it acts as a powerful assistant—much like a good teacher who guides you through the process. Consensus won't take over the writing, but it will make the task significantly easier. By asking the right questions, you can extract valuable insights, break down complex topics, and advance your work effectively.

This tutorial will guide you on how to use Consensus to enhance your Literature Review or Dissertation writing process, helping you navigate the complexities of academic research.

## Key points in this guide

- Do not use Consensus to write the review or dissertation for you: Instead, use it as an academic assistant.

- Use Consensus for assistance across all sections: It helps with the overall structure and each part of the Literature Review or Dissertation.

- Consensus helps build a reading foundation: Use it to break large topics into smaller, manageable sections.

- Utilize prompts for Consensus Pro: Maximize your searches with specific prompts for different purposes (e.g., research questions, outlines).

- Ask Consensus about limitations in the evidence: This ensures balanced and nuanced insights.

- Save key searches and papers: Create lists for quick reference during your writing process.


## Quick start prompts

This section provides a collection of practical prompts designed to help you make the most of Consensus during your research and writing process. These prompts can guide you through various stages, from generating research questions to outlining your literature review and finding key references. Whether you're seeking to clarify your topic, organize your ideas, or identify gaps in the literature, these prompts will serve as a starting point to streamline your research. By leveraging these targeted prompts, you can more efficiently navigate the complexities of academic writing and produce well-informed, comprehensive work.

### Generate potential review questions about your topic

\[button text="Provide some research questions for a literature review around obesity that currently do not exist"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide some research questions that require compare and contrast around obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide research questions that require pro and cons around obesity"\]\[/button\]

### Gauging the review question content for interest, scope, and difficulty

"What should be discussed when writing about \[your topic\]"

\[button text="What should be discussed when writing about obesity"\]\[/button\]

### Construct the review outline and format

"Provide an outline for a literature review about the \[review format\] of \[topic\]"

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about the benefits and risks of obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about the quality of evidence on obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about the mechanisms of obesity"\]\[/button\]

### Construct the content for each subheading of the review

"Provide an outline for a literature review about \[subheading 1 from Consensus Pro output for 'Construct the review outline'\]"

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about genetic factors contributing to obesity"\]\[/button\]

### Find definitions and concepts

"What key concepts do I need to know about around \[topic\]?"

\[button text="What key concepts do I need to know about around obesity?"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide a discussion of 10 important concepts about obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Give me definitions for key terms relating to obesity"\]\[/button\]

### The flexibility to learn according to your needs and style

"Provide \[notes, bullet points, a summary\] on \[topic\]"

\[button text="Provide notes on obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide bullet points on obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide a summary on obesity"\]\[/button\]

### Critical appraisal: Nuance and variation

"How does \[variable\] vary by \[variable/factor\]"

\[button text="How does obesity vary by country?"\]\[/button\]

"How does \[variable\] vary between \[variable 1\] and \[variable 2\]?"

\[button text="How does obesity vary between adults and children?"\]\[/button\]

### Strengths and limitations

"What are the limitations and strengths of \[topic, method, study design\]"

\[button text="What are the limitations and strengths of case-control studies?"\]\[/button\]

### Find whether a theory or idea is supported

"Is there any evidence to support the following statement: \[statement\]"

\[button text="Is there any evidence to support the following statement: withdrawing tirzepatide leads to substantial regain of lost weight?"\]\[/button\]

### Find references to support a specific claim

"Find me references which support the following statement: \[statement\]"

\[button text="Find me references which support the following statement: “observational cohort studies show that obesity is associated with lower income in high income countries ”"\]\[/button\]

### Future research needs

"What are the unanswered questions regarding \[topic\]?"

\[button text="What are the unanswered questions regarding obesity?"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="What research is needed to better understand obesity?"\]\[/button\]

### Implications

"What are the \[policy, practical, public health\] implications of \[topic\]?"

\[button text="What are the policy implications of evidence on genetics and obesity?"\]\[/button\]

### Find other viewpoints

"Provide the existing opposing viewpoints that do not support the statement that \[claim\]"

\[button text="Provide the existing opposing viewpoints that do not support the statement that personalized medicine will solve the obesity epidemic"\]\[/button\]

### Finding papers based on experimental/observational study design

"Reference the studies closest matching this study design: population = \[e.g. adults, children, diabetes\], intervention = \[e.g. a drug, a diet, a support programme, any\], comparison = \[e.g. a control, placebo, current gold-standard treatment, any\], outcome = \[e.g. death, recovery from disease, blood pressure, any\]"

\[button text="Reference the studies closest matching this study design: population = obesity, intervention = semaglutide, comparison = any, outcome = weight loss"\]\[/button\]

## Developing your Literature Review or Dissertation question

Depending on your course, you may already have a set research question, but many institutions allow you to choose your own. Deciding on an interesting and valuable question can be challenging, but Consensus Pro can help. By exploring your subject area or specific interests, you can use Consensus to generate relevant research questions based on the latest literature. This ensures your question is both informed and aligned with current academic discussions. For example:

\[button text="Provide some research questions for a literature review around obesity that currently do not exist"\]\[/button\]

If you have a better idea of the type of question you wish to address, you can also add this in the specifics of the essay type. You could compare and contrast, or consider pros and cons:

\[button text="Provide research questions that require compare and contrast around obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide research questions that require pro and cons around obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[embed\]https://youtu.be/4Ov7wBarspI\[/embed\]

### Understanding your question topic

If you’ve been handed a question, you may be needing some help in understanding your topic and getting to grips with the literature. Your teacher/lecturer may also have given you a lengthy reading list of books, of which only 5% will be relevant. Let Consensus get you up to speed on the topic by understanding key concepts by finding the most important/references to get you started on reading:

\[button text="What key concepts do I need to know about around obesity"\]\[/button\]

Consensus will put key references at the top of your page. Without having to apply any filters, Consensus provided a Nature Reviews Primer, European and Canadian Guidelines, and two papers providing definitions, prevalence rates, causes and treatments, perfect to get you up to speed. Or, save yourself even more time, and turn on Consensus **Pro** and repeat the search:

![](https://framerusercontent.com/images/NqcPUAlDaUl71Qh1VOmGWYKFn8.png)

You can also use the find concepts prompt below for a simpler breakdown:

\[button text="Provide a discussion of 10 important concepts about obesity"\]\[/button\]

Or, you may be given a list of questions to choose from. You want to make sure that the question you choose and the content involved will be interesting or within your scope/ability. Again, type in each question to get a flavour for what you may need to research.

"What should be discussed when writing about \[question\]"

\[button text="What should be discussed when writing about the benefits and drawbacks of weight reduction interventions in older adults with obesity?"\]\[/button\]

Now, you have a quick overview of your topic, and key references to get you started.

## Saving your reference list & key searches

At this stage, it’s a good idea to set up your list of useful papers and searches. The advantage of this is that:

1. You can quickly refer to key papers, and use **Ask paper** to ask questions

2. You can quickly export all the references you need to your reference software

3. You can quickly re-enter the search at a later date without having to re-type or remember the exact words, and easily apply filters or tweaks to the search


Check out our [guide on saving lists of search and paper results in Consensus](https://consensus.app/home/blog/save-lists-of-searches-and-papers-in-consensus/). Later, we’ll show you [how you can quickly export your references into your reference software](https://consensus.app/home/blog/integrate-consensus-with-reference-managers-zotero-and-more/).

### Creating the outline structure and topics to research

Now we have our question, initial literature and key concepts ready to begin writing. Next, we can assist our outline of the literature review, to make sure we address all relevant aspects. For example, let’s say our title was about genetic factors and environmental factors in obesity. We could ask:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about “How do genetic factors compare to environmental factors in their contribution to obesity?”"\]\[/button\]

\[embed\]https://youtu.be/C4l5x-Fxhxk\[/embed\]

You now have a clear structure to answer the question, with subheadings and brief outlines of the key sections you need to consider in your writing. You can also see that in each section/subheading in the outline will also include key references. You can use these as a starting point for your reading. Make sure to save these references to your list for later. You can also export the Consensus Pro output into your word processor, ready to begin research and writing. Make sure to consider the type of essay you are writing when performing your search, as this will alter the response provided and the outline of the review. For example:

Compare and contrast:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about “How are genetic factors similar or different to environmental factors in their contribution to obesity?”"\]\[/button\]

Mechanisms:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about the mechanisms of genetic factors and environmental factors contributing to obesity"\]\[/button\]

Knowledge-based / discussion:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about what is known about genetic factors and environmental factors contributing to obesity"\]\[/button\]

Argumentative:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about determining which is more important in contributing to obesity: genetic factors or environmental factors?"\]\[/button\]

Pros and cons:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about the pros and cons of studying genetic factors and environmental factors contributing to obesity"\]\[/button\]

For the obesity example, the first few sections are relatively similar, as they introduce the topic and discuss genetic and environmental factors. But, the latter sections are tailored to the essay type in question. Use this to your advantage to ensure you structure your essay correctly.

"Provide an outline for a literature review about the \[review format, e.g.benefits and risks, mechanisms, pros and cons\] of \[topic\]"

You can extract this structure and use it as your starting point to write your review in your word processor (e.g., Google Docs or Microsoft Word).

## Building your review

Now you have your outline, you can take each of the sections outlined in the last search, and use these as new searches in Consensus. By using targeted searches, you can gather detailed information and relevant references for every section of your review. Consensus allows you to break down complex topics into manageable parts, ensuring you cover all critical areas comprehensively. This approach will help you create a well-organized, thoroughly researched, and academically sound review. For example, take the genetic and environmental factors in obesity example above:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about “How do genetic factors compare to environmental factors in their contribution to obesity?”"\]\[/button\]

We can ask about the second subheading from the Consensus Pro output:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about Genetic Factors Contributing to Obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[embed\]https://youtu.be/nD23iyXyNhg\[/embed\]

You can continually do this to hone in on specific topics and get into highly detailed literature, all from a broad start. For example, take a subheading from the Consensus Pro output for the Genetic Factors Contributing to Obesity search above:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about Monogenic Obesity"\]\[/button\]

Within a couple of clicks, we have a highly detailed picture from an initially broad overview, with details on monogenic forms of obesity. You can repeat the above process for each subheading to get more and more granular in terms of detail. As you may guess, we could identify even more about monogenic obesity by searching with another subheading:

\[button text="Provide an outline for a literature review about Syndromic vs. Non-Syndromic Monogenic Obesity"\]\[/button\]

Make sure to also use the related searches beneath the references to expand your thinking and generate more insightful results, building out each of your topics.

![](https://framerusercontent.com/images/cX8adjCTWicBvErrF5jBdiN0oY.png)

This quick, yet incredibly powerful tip allows you to explore and find topics within your essay that you may not have even been aware of. From just a single word (in this case, “obesity”), we can map out the underlying structure of the topic (see figure above). This will also allow you to better conceptually understand your question and ultimately, write a much more comprehensive review.

Next, we will cover some useful tips when constructing each section of the review.

### Definitions and meanings

For most written pieces, the introduction will include definitions of key terms. We can use Consensus to help us here:

\[button text="Give me definitions for key terms relating to genetics and environment in their contribution to obesity"\]\[/button\]

You will now be given a set of key terms and definitions relating to your topic, along with the reference for it, so you can correctly cite in your review. Make sure to save these references to your list. If you’re unsure about a definition, you can put it back into the Search to learn a bit more. For example, if you were unsure on what a gene-environment interaction is:

\[button text="Tell me about Gene-Environment Interaction, what does it mean"\]\[/button\]

You can use this prompt for extra definitions relating to your later sections, for example:

\[button text="Give me definitions for key terms relating to global prevalence and health impacts of obesity"\]\[/button\]

### Obtaining key summary data

Depending on the review and suggestions for your review outline created earlier, your introduction may also need some strong summary data or key facts. In our obesity review, we should include data on disease prevalence:

\[button text="What is the prevalence of obesity?"\]\[/button\]

Make sure to apply the **Published since** date filter to get recent estimates for prevalence:

Repeat this for other key data points, such as the social impact or cost of obesity.

### Writing out the review

We can then move onto the next section of the introduction:

\[button text="Provide an introduction to the contributing factors to obesity"\]\[/button\]

The advantage of Consensus is that you can obtain this information in the format that most suits your learning style:

"Provide \[notes, bullet points, a summary\] on \[topic\], in simple words"

\[button text="Provide notes on the contributing factors to obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide bullet points on the contributing factors to obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide a summary on the contributing factors to obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide information on the contributing factors to obesity in simple words"\]\[/button\]

\[embed\]https://youtu.be/dENWglzxbNM\[/embed\]

We are now using the subheading sections identified in the Consensus Pro outputs from the 'building your review' section as new searches in Consensus to start adding in detail. We can repeat this for each of our subheadings, ensuring that we have a framework of insights and references for each of our sections. With this, we can begin to write our review in our word processing document.

### Critical appraisal and discussion

Now, the difficult part. Regardless of your essay topic or type, at some point you’ll need to gather all of the points and thoughts you have laid out so far into a discussion. For example, whether we need to critically appraise, compare or contrast ideas, weigh up pros and cons, or argue for a given conclusion, critical thinking sets apart the top thinkers from the rest.

Utilize consensus to help you with your thinking, to help you analyze papers, clarify support for a claim, identify multiple academic viewpoints on a topic, identify strengths and weaknesses in the evidence, and suggest further research or policy/practical implications of the review findings.

## Discovering nuance and context

Now we have structure to our review, we can start to search and consider nuance and context. For example, whether prevalence, outcomes or treatments differ based on demographics, country, or study design:

"How does \[variable\] vary by \[variable\]?"

\[button text="How does obesity treatment availability vary by country"\]\[/button\]

"How does \[variable\] vary between \[variable 1\] and \[variable 2\]?"

\[button text="How does obesity treatment vary between males and females"\]\[/button\]

Identifying nuance can help to provide a detailed and insightful review.

### Analyzing a paper

You’ll need to discuss important research and studies in your review. You can use the new **Ask paper** feature to help you understand each key paper. Ask to summarize the findings, explore the methods used, explain complex terms, and more. Turn on **Ask Paper available** to only be provided with references where you can use it.

![Analyzing a paper](https://framerusercontent.com/images/zrcybRSlaZoxeqsEr4GOfm0ctI.png)

## Finding references to support a claim or idea

Perhaps you’ve read in a paper about a theory or idea by another academic, but they did not provide a citation, or, your reading has led you to a thought or a new idea. But is it backed by any evidence? We can use Consensus to get an answer in seconds:

\[button text="Is there any evidence to support the following statement: withdrawing tirzepatide leads to substantial regain of lost weight"\]\[/button\]

If we put in the opposite statement, we can see that Consensus informs us that the statement is not supported, with strong evidence against this:

\[button text="Is there any evidence to support the following statement: withdrawing tirzepatide leads to weight loss"\]\[/button\]

It can therefore be a good idea to enter both prompts, which can allow you to build up a picture of the for and against arguments for a statement. You can also use this prompt to find a specific reference, such as if you find that you have made some statements that are not supported by a reference, and you cannot remember where the statement originated from:

\[button text="Find me references which support the following statement: “observational cohort studies show that obesity is associated with lower income in high income countries”"\]\[/button\]

This can work very well if you are looking for references for a statement within a specific niche or context. If there are no directly relevant papers, Consensus will provide this in the output, with the next most relevant papers, with an explanation why. For example:

\[button text="Find me references to support the following statement: prospective studies show that abdominal obesity is associated with living alone in high-income countries"\]\[/button\]

Remember, this prompt does not address whether the statement is generally well supported. It may be that there are only one or two references supporting this, compared to many references supporting the opposite statement. So, make sure not to use this prompt out of context, and be aware of the overall literature.

### Obtain multiple viewpoints for the discussion

In academia, some topics have an overarching general consensus and agreement, whereas others may be more inconclusive. At the boundaries of existing knowledge, there can be a number of ideas or theories. A key aspect of any review is ensuring that the range of views in the literature are considered in proportion to the amount of supporting evidence. Of course, there may be other academic views that you’ve not considered or are unaware of. Again, Consensus can help with identifying these:

\[button text="What are the different viewpoints regarding personalized medicine as the best option to treat obesity?"\]\[/button\]

### Dealing with our own confirmation bias

Good scientists are willing to change their stance or opinion on a topic, given new evidence that contradicts it. Of course, as we become more familiar and more knowledgeable on a topic, we may well form strong beliefs, that, perhaps, we should be less confident, following further publications. We can use Consensus to help us to identify and consider conflicting viewpoints to our own, with limitations in and contrasting evidence against our belief:

\[button text="Provide the existing opposing viewpoints that do not support the statement that personalized medicine is the best option to treat obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Provide the existing viewpoints that do not support the statement that exercise helps with weight loss"\]\[/button\]

As we start to draw our own conclusions, this approach to writing a review can help us to be good academics and researchers, and build a more critical review.

### Identifying strengths and limitations in the evidence

An important aspect of academic work is acknowledging the strengths and weaknesses of a piece of work. We could do this for the quality of evidence on a topic, a type of measurement tool (e.g. ELISA, a questionnaire), a trial or cohort. We can use Consensus to help us identify what these may be.

\[button text="Summarize the limitations and strengths of the evidence regarding genetics and obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="Summarize the limitations and strengths of the evidence regarding the environment and obesity"\]\[/button\]

We can now discuss these insights in the review, and as above, further investigate the points of most interest. We can use this prompt in other ways, such as to assess specific research methods:

\[button text="What are the limitations and strengths of mendelian randomisation studies to assess genetic factors in obesity?"\]\[/button\]

To assess study designs:

\[button text="What are the limitations and strengths of cohort studies to assess the environmental factors in obesity?"\]\[/button\]

Or to assess specific studies or cohorts:

\[button text="What are the limitations and strengths of the UK BioBank to assess the environmental factors in obesity?"\]\[/button\]

We advise performing a combined search of both limitations and strengths in a single search, to provide a balanced response.

### Unanswered questions and further research

Few fields are closed books. Even as a new study comes in with conclusive results, it often opens up more questions than it answers. Therefore any good review will include research recommendations to address the unanswered questions.

\[button text="What are the unanswered questions regarding the environmental factors contributing to obesity?"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="What research is needed to better understand the environmental factors contributing to obesity?"\]\[/button\]

### Policy/practical implications of the review findings

Any good review also provides a summary of what the literature and its findings mean for policy or practice.

\[button text="What are the policy implications of the environmental factors contributing to obesity "\]\[/button\]

\[button text="What are the practical implications of the genetic factors contributing to obesity"\]\[/button\]

\[button text="What are the practical implications of the environmental factors contributing to obesity"\]\[/button\]

## References: Finding key, extra, or specific papers

Depending on your essay, you may need to find some primary research papers (e.g. a clinical trial or an observational study, rather than a review). Use these helpful tips to find relevant papers for your review.

### Find a specific trial or observational study based on its design

A key outline of a study is PICO: population, intervention/exposure, comparator/control, and outcome. We can use this structure in our search to find studies meeting this design. For example:

\[button text="Reference the studies closest matching this study design: population = adults with obesity, intervention = semaglutide, comparison = placebo, outcome = weight"\]\[/button\]

Make sure to use the **Study types** filter for an observational study or experimental study for more specific insights.

### Find high-impact papers

You can find prominent papers in your topic in two ways. One, apply a minimum citation number in the **Citations** filter:

![Find high-impact papers ](https://framerusercontent.com/images/f6Fq1FwNwuRHDKlBbLGQ7DSjCro.png)

The number of citations of a high-impact paper will vary by subject and age of the publication, but generally, papers with at least 50-100 citations are pretty important, and anything over 500-1000 are very important.

Two, select from high-quality journals only using the **Journals** SJR quartile rating filter:

![Select from high-quality journals only](https://framerusercontent.com/images/FxcG2E4RFe2d7NYJ7oUAJqTVzas.png)

### Snowballing: check citations of older/high-impact papers

Look through the citations of one of the key papers from your searches to find recent relevant studies. This works particularly well for papers at least a few years old, or highly influential papers. An easy way to find these is to filter by the number of citations to find high impact and influential papers.

![](https://framerusercontent.com/images/M8CNSNpDmg9q7bytrSMQJbHatwo.png)

\[embed\]https://youtu.be/5FUy6SOAlcE\[/embed\]

#### Check references of key/recent papers

Look at the references of one of the key papers from your searches to find older relevant studies. This often works well for more recent papers, such as those with specific study designs that you wish to find more of, as they will often cite any similar previous studies, as well as any important papers.

![](https://framerusercontent.com/images/QoLCDrzqtPdv0AKYGBKby0aSI.png)

\[embed\]https://youtu.be/cKrjInc4cmQ\[/embed\]

### Exporting references from Consensus to your reference software

References - make use of lists. Along the way, make sure to save the useful references from your searches to a list, and also save useful searches. If you want to be really organised, you can even create a list for each section of your review.

[Save all to a list and then export references to Zotero, or download as CSV or RIS files](https://consensus.app/home/blog/integrate-consensus-with-reference-managers-zotero-and-more/). On the page of a paper, you can also click on the Zotero plugin to automatically save that paper. Or, if you see a useful reference during your searches, just click on the Zotero button or export to quickly add it to your reference software.

![Exporting references from Consensus to your reference software](https://framerusercontent.com/images/Yeq6ashI33zZWpYTFcDDfnHpA.png)![Exporting references from Consensus to your reference software](https://framerusercontent.com/images/WNx0x9lIo7RDPTHKwiYsAsr1g4.png)![](https://framerusercontent.com/images/QMl4Hvhryfhyck8tPkSaeUJQQg.png)

You can then easily use the reference in your document (i.e. Google Docs or Word).

## Summary

In summary, Consensus is a powerful tool for assisting with writing a literature review or dissertation by providing structured support across the entire process. Consensus can help with generating potential review questions, constructing the review outline, finding key definitions and references, and constructing the content for each section of the review, including key aspects such as critical appraisal, future research needs and implications, all while upholding academic standards. By using the prompts in this guide, Consensus enables users to build a comprehensive understanding of their topic, identifying gaps in research, and critically assessing evidence. Consensus can help to explore alternative viewpoints, obtain nuanced insights, and recognize strengths and limitations in studies. Additionally, users can save searches and key references for easier citation management and organization. While Consensus won’t write the review for you, it acts as a valuable support throughout the writing process, making complex academic research more accessible and manageable. With careful application of its features, Consensus can support the development of well-informed and academically sound writing.

By asking the right questions, you can extract valuable insights, break down complex topics, and advance your work effectively.

# The Ultimate Guide to Writing a Literature Review

Master how to write a literature review: search, synthesize, and structure prior studies with frameworks, templates, and examples you can use today.

Oct 27, 2025— [Prudhvi](https://scispace.com/resources/author/prudhvi/ "Prudhvi")

![The Ultimate Guide to Writing a Literature Review](https://scispace.com/resources/content/images/size/w2400/2025/10/Literature-Review-Guide.png)

## The Ultimate Guide to Writing a Literature Review

Struggling with **writing a literature review** that does more than summarize articles? This ultimate guide keeps your original structure and expands each section with proven frameworks (PRISMA, CARS, SALSA), templates, worked passages, and checklists. The goal is simple: help you move from scattered notes to a _coherent, critical synthesis_ that sets up your study and stands up to academic scrutiny.

[AI Literature Review Generator](https://scispace.com/agents/literature-review-generator-4f7bnanl?ref=scispace.com)

[Systematic Literature Generator (PRISMA)](https://scispace.com/agents/systematic-literature-review-prisma-4b297816?ref=scispace.com)

## What Is a Literature Review?

A literature review surveys and _synthesizes_ scholarly sources about a focused question to contextualize your work. Done well, it maps debates, reveals gaps, and justifies your study—not by listing summaries, but by **building claims** with converging evidence.

1. **Identify the purpose**: standalone assignment, dissertation chapter, grant background, or journal article section.
2. **Define scope**: population, phenomenon, timeframe, geography, methods included/excluded.
3. **Know the types**: narrative/traditional, _scoping_, _systematic_, _rapid_, _umbrella_, _realist_; choose based on the decision you must inform.
4. **Emphasize synthesis**: compare/contrast, evaluate quality, integrate patterns and mechanisms; avoid article-by-article summaries.
5. **Practice criticality**: assess internal validity, external validity, consistency, and applicability.

## Why Literature Reviews Are Important

Great reviews are decision tools: they establish what is known, what remains uncertain, and where your study fits.

1. **Build credibility**: show command of canonical works and the latest findings.
2. **Justify novelty**: name specific gaps (population, setting, method, timeframe, theory).
3. **Avoid duplication**: ensure your study adds unique value rather than replicating past work without reason.
4. **Inform design**: adopt best measures, sample frames, and analytic strategies from prior studies.
5. **Bridge disciplines**: connect ideas across fields (e.g., psychology + HCI) for richer explanations.

## Steps to Conduct a Literature Review (Searching & Selecting Literature)

Gather sources systematically so your synthesis rests on transparent, reproducible methods.

1. **Pose a focused question**: frameworks like [PICO](https://scispace.com/agents/pico-generator-bwy89on0?ref=scispace.com)/PICOS (Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcomes, Study design) for quantitative work, or SPIDER (Sample, Phenomenon, Design, Evaluation, Research type) for qualitative.
2. **Build search strings**: synonyms + Boolean operators + truncation. Example: `(adolescen* OR teen*) AND (sleep OR circadian) AND (greenspace OR park* OR tree*)`.
3. **Search multiple databases**: [Google Scholar](https://scispace.com/agents/google-scholar-454ec92a?ref=scispace.com)(breadth), [PubMed](https://scispace.com/agents/pubmed-h7k5amey?ref=scispace.com)/MEDLINE, Scopus, Web of Science, PsycINFO, [ERIC](https://scispace.com/agents/eric-search-kkqbj3jy?ref=scispace.com); include key conference proceedings if relevant.
4. **Grey literature**: theses, reports, preprints (selectively, note limitations). Hand-search reference lists and forward-cite seminal studies.
5. **Document decisions**: record search dates, strings, filters, databases; store in a log or spreadsheet for transparency.

![PRISMA-style flow: visualize the path from identification to inclusion. Replace XXX with your counts.](https://scispace.com/resources/content/images/2025/10/prisma_flow_diagram.png)_PRISMA-style flow_: visualize the path from identification to inclusion. Replace `XXX` with your counts.

[Generate PRISMA Flow Diagram with AI](https://scispace.com/agents/chart-maker-prisma-flow-ydhv8fkm?ref=scispace.com)

| Stage | Action | Evidence to save | Tooling |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Search | Run strings across 3–5 databases | Dates, strings, filters | [SciSpace Google Scholar Agent](https://scispace.com/agents/google-scholar-454ec92a%20target=?ref=scispace.com) |
| Screen | Title/abstract screening | Include/exclude notes | Spreadsheet or Rayyan |
| Eligibility | Full-text assessment | Reasons for exclusion | [SciSpace SLR Full Text Screening Agent](https://scispace.com/agents/systematic-literature-review-full-text-screening-wzkpuvuo?ref=scispace.com) |
| Extraction | Pull data/quotes/tables | Standardized sheet | [SLR Extract Data](https://scispace.com/agents/systematic-literature-review-data-extraction-9v9o6d8e?ref=scispace.com) |

## Organizing and Structuring Your Literature Review

Readers need a map. Choose a structure that matches your research logic and the state of the field.

1. **Thematic** (recommended): organize around _concepts_ or _mechanisms_, not authors. Each theme becomes a mini-argument.
2. **Chronological**: use sparingly; emphasize how theory/methods evolved rather than listing studies by year.
3. **Methodological**: group by design (RCT, longitudinal, ethnography); ideal when method explains divergent findings.
4. **Hybrid**: e.g., chronological within themes to show evolution inside each concept.
5. **Outlining**: present a clear H2/H3 hierarchy; avoid mixing criteria at the same level.

| Structure | Best when | Pitfall | Fix |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Thematic | Field has converging clusters | Theme drift | Start each theme with a topic sentence + boundary |
| Chronological | Historical development is key | List-like narrative | Explain “what changed and why” at period breaks |
| Methodological | Method drives outcomes | Overemphasis on technique | Tie methods back to theory and findings |

## Writing the Literature Review (Introduction, Body, Conclusion)

Turn piles of PDFs into a persuasive narrative with paragraph‑level discipline.

1. **Introduction**: scope, definitions, and a map of themes; end with a claim about the field (e.g., “Evidence is mixed due to measurement heterogeneity”).
2. **Body**: synthesis paragraphs—not summaries. Use the _TS‑SYN‑EVID‑CRIT‑LINK_ pattern:

- **TS** (topic sentence): the theme’s claim.
- **SYN**: integrate 2–4 sources (agreement/contrast).
- **EVID**: representative data or effect sizes.
- **CRIT**: limitations, bias risks, boundary conditions.
- **LINK**: transition forward or back to the research question.

1. **Conclusion**: state settled knowledge, live debates, and _specific_ gaps your study will fill.
2. **Referencing**: keep citations consistent; add a running bibliography as you draft.
3. **Voice**: precise, synthesis-first; avoid “study-by-study” reporting.

| Theme | Mini-claim (TS) | Evidence synthesis | Limitations/critique | Next link |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Greenspace & sleep | Greenspace correlates with longer adolescent sleep | 3 cohort studies; pooled β≈+0.10 h/10pp greenspace | Self-report sleep; urban confounding | Mechanisms: noise, heat, stress |

## Examples of Effective Literature Review Passages

Model paragraphs that demonstrate synthesis, critique, and flow.

1. **Synthesis (agreement + nuance)**: “Across three cohort studies, neighborhood greenspace positively predicts weekday sleep in adolescents (β≈0.10–0.15 h/10pp). However, estimates shrink when adjusting for night‑time noise indices, suggesting partial mediation by acoustic environments.”
2. **Contradiction handling**: “While two RCTs of blue‑light filters report modest benefits, a cross-over trial found no effect; differences in baseline chronotype and adherence likely explain the discrepancy.”
3. **Mechanism bridging**: “Effects concentrate in heat‑prone neighborhoods, aligning with thermal stress recovery models.”
4. **Boundary conditions**: “Associations vanish in rural samples, implying urban mechanisms (noise, light-at-night) drive observed effects.”
5. **Gap sharpening**: “Few studies combine actigraphy with high‑resolution land-cover data; this measurement gap limits causal inference.”

## Tips for a Comprehensive Literature Review

Scale your workflow without drowning in PDFs.

1. **Update regularly**: schedule a last‑7‑days database alert to capture late additions.
2. **Use tools**: [SciSpace ChatPDF](https://scispace.com/chat-pdf?ref=scispace.com) for rapid extraction; [Extract Data](https://scispace.com/agents/systematic-literature-review-data-extraction-9v9o6d8e?ref=scispace.com) for tables/figures; [PRISMA assistants](https://scispace.com/agents/systematic-literature-review-prisma-4b297816?ref=scispace.com) to build flow diagrams.
3. **Mitigate bias**: predefine inclusion criteria; log deviations; screen in pairs when possible.
4. **Timebox**: adopt 45‑min reading sprints + 15‑min synthesis writes to convert reading into prose.
5. **Archive**: store PDFs, notes, and extraction sheets in a consistent folder structure; version your outline.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid in Literature Reviews

Prevent reviewer pushback by avoiding these traps.

1. **Over‑summarizing** (one paragraph per paper) → switch to theme‑first synthesis.
2. **Ignoring contradictions** → explain divergences (population, measures, design).
3. **Poor organization** → use explicit headings and signposts; maintain parallelism.
4. **Neglecting quality** → appraise risk of bias (CASP, ROBINS‑I, AMSTAR‑2) and indicate certainty (GRADE).
5. **Outdated set** → refresh searches; mark “search last updated: YYYY‑MM‑DD.”

## Citing Sources & Avoiding Plagiarism

Academic integrity underpins your review. Keep citation style consistent (APA/MLA/Chicago). Cite claims, define borrowed terms, and paraphrase faithfully. For quick lookups and formatting, consult institutional guides and use managers (Zotero, EndNote).

## Final Checklist

- Clear research question + explicit scope
- Transparent search strings, databases, dates
- Inclusion/exclusion criteria applied consistently
- Themes/methods organized into a logical hierarchy
- Synthesis paragraphs with critique, not summaries
- Quality appraisal signaled where relevant
- Gaps + contribution articulated
- References complete and consistent

## FAQ

### How long should a literature review be?

Anywhere from 2,000 to 10,000+ words depending on project scope and venue. Target depth and synthesis quality over raw length.

### What is the difference between a literature review and a systematic review?

A literature review synthesizes studies narratively; a systematic review follows pre‑registered methods (e.g., PRISMA) to minimize bias and may include meta‑analysis.

### How do I find sources efficiently?

Start with recent reviews to map the field; mine their references and use forward citation tracking. Combine database strategies with curated alerts.

### Can I include non‑peer‑reviewed sources?

Use cautiously (reports, theses, preprints) and flag the limitation; prioritize peer‑reviewed evidence for claims.

### How do I write critically rather than descriptively?

Make a claim per paragraph, support it with converging sources, expose limitations, and connect back to your research question.

### What tools actually save time?

Use SciSpace tools for reading/extraction, a reference manager for citations, and a template for TS‑SYN‑EVID‑CRIT‑LINK paragraphs to convert reading into writing.

# Automate your literature review with AI

Traditional methods of literature review can be limiting. Thanks to artificial intelligence, long and tedious literature reviews are becoming quick and comprehensive to the point where researchers don't have to spend endless hours combing through stacks of books and journals.

Sep 30, 2023— [Shubham Dogra](https://scispace.com/resources/author/shubham/ "Shubham Dogra")

![Automate your literature review with AI](https://scispace.com/resources/content/images/size/w2400/2023/09/SM-final-route-34.png)

## Automate your literature review with AI

Traditional methods of literature review can be [susceptible to errors](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41559-020-01295-x?ref=scispace.com). Whether it’s overcoming [human bias](https://scispace.com/resources/literature-review-with-ai/%3Ca%20href=%22www.nature.com/articles/s41559-020-01295-x%22%20target=%22_blank%22%3Ehuman%20bias%3C/a%3E) or sifting through an incredibly [large amount of scientific research](https://www.cnet.com/science/there-are-too-many-scientific-studies-says-scientific-study/?ref=scispace.com) being published today. Not to forget all the papers that have already been published in the past 100 years. Putting both together makes a heap of information that is humanly impossible to sift through. At least do so in an efficient way.

Thanks to artificial intelligence, long and tedious literature reviews are becoming quick and comprehensive. No longer do researchers have to spend endless hours combing through stacks of books and journals. They can just quickly [Chat with their PDFs](https://scispace.com/chat-pdf?ref=scispace.com) using AI!

In this blog post, we'll dive deep into the world of automating your literature review with AI, Chat PDF reader, exploring what a literature review is, why it's so crucial, and how you can harness AI tools to make the process more effective.

## **What is a literature review?**

A literature review is essentially the foundation of a scientific research project, providing a comprehensive overview of existing knowledge on a specific topic. It gives an overview of your chosen topic and summarizes key findings, theories, and methodologies from various sources.

This critical analysis not only showcases the current state of understanding but also identifies gaps and trends in the scientific literature. In addition, it also shows your understanding of your field and can help provide credibility to your [research paper](https://scispace.com/resources/research-paper-introduction/).

## **Types of literature review**

There are several types of literature reviews but for the most part, you will come across five versions. These are:

**1\. Narrative review:** A narrative review provides a comprehensive overview of a topic, usually without a strict methodology for selection.

**2\. Systematic review:** Systematic reviews are a strategic synthesis of a topic. This type of review follows a strict plan to identify, evaluate, and critique all relevant research on a topic to minimize bias.

**3\. Meta-analysis:** It is a type of systematic review that uses research data from multiple articles to draw quantitative conclusions about a specific phenomenon.

**4\. Scoping review:** As the name suggests, the purpose of a [scoping review](https://scispace.com/resources/evaluating-literature-review-systematic-review-vs-scoping-review/) is to study a field, highlight the gaps in it, and underline the need for the following research paper.

**5\. Critical review:** A critical literature review assesses and critiques the strengths and weaknesses of existing literature, challenging established ideas and theories.

## **Benefits of using literature review AI tools?**

Using literature review AI tools can be a complete game changer in your research. They can make the literature review process smarter and hassle-free. Here are some practical benefits:

### Efficiency

AI tools for literature review can skim through tons of research papers and find the most relevant one for your topic in no time, thus saving you hours of manual searching.

### Comprehensive insights

No matter how complex the topic is or how long the research papers are, AI tools can find key insights like methodology, datasets, limitations, etc, by simply scanning the abstracts or PDF documents.

### Eliminate bias

AI doesn't have favorites. Based on the data it’s fed, it evaluates research papers objectively and reduces as much bias in your literature review as possible.

### Faster research questions

AI tools present loads of research papers in the same place. Some AI tools let you create visual maps and connections, thus helping you identify gaps in existing literature and arriving at your [research question](https://scispace.com/resources/how-to-write-a-research-question/) faster.

### Consistency

AI tools ensure your review is consistently [structured and formatted](https://scispace.com/resources/literature-review-structure/). They can also check for proper grammar and citation style, which is crucial for scholarly writing.

### Multilingual support

There are heaps of non-native English-speaking researchers who can [struggle](https://theconversation.com/non-native-english-speaking-scientists-work-much-harder-just-to-keep-up-global-research-reveals-208750?ref=scispace.com) with understanding scientific jargon in English. AI tools with multilingual support can help such academicians conduct their literature review in their own language.

## **How to write a literature review with AI**

Now that we understand the benefits of a literature review using artificial intelligence, let's explore how you can automate the process. Literature reviews with [AI-powered tools](https://scispace.com/resources/online-tools-for-easy-literature-review/) can save you countless hours and allow a more comprehensive and systematic approach.

Here's one process you can follow:

### Choose the right AI tool

Several AI search engines like Google Scholar, SciSpace [ChatPDF](https://scispace.com/resources/best-chatpdf-tool-scispace/), Semantic Scholar help you find the most relevant papers semantically. Or in other words even without the right keywords. These tools understand the context of your search query and deliver the results.

### Find relevant research papers

Once you input your research question or keywords into a search engine like Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, or SciSpace, it scours millions of papers worth of databases to find relevant articles. After that, you can narrow your search results to a certain time period, journals, number of citations, and other parameters for more accuracy.

### Analyze the search results

Now that you have your list of relevant academic papers, the next step would be reviewing these results. A lot of AI-powered tools for literature review will often provide summaries along with the paper.

Some sophisticated tools also help you gather key points from multiple papers at once and let you ask questions regarding that topic. This way, you can get an understanding of the topic and further have a better understanding of your field.

### Organize your collection

Whether you’re writing a literature review or your paper, you will need to keep track of your references. Using AI tools, you can efficiently organize your findings, store them in reference managers, and instantly generate citations automatically, saving you the hassle of manually formatting references.

### Write the literature review

Now that you’ve done your groundwork, you can start writing your literature review. Although you should be doing this yourself, you can use tools like paraphrasers, grammar checkers, and co-writers to help you refine your academic writing and get your point across with more clarity.

## **Best AI Tools for Literature Review**

Since generative AI and ChatGPT came into the picture, there are heaps of AI tools for literature review available out there. Some of the most comprehensive ones are:

### SciSpace

[SciSpace](https://scispace.com/resources/scispace-literature-review-workspace/) is a valuable tool to have in your arsenal. It has a repository of 270M+ papers and makes it easy to find research articles. You can also extract key information to compare and contrast multiple papers at the same time. Then, go on to converse with individual papers using [SciSpace ChatPDF](https://scispace.com/resources/smallpdf-vs-scispace-chatpdf/), your AI research assistant.

> Love using SciSpace tools? Enjoy discounts!
>
> Use SR40 (40% off yearly) and SR20 (20% off monthly).
>
> Claim yours here 👉 [SciSpace Premium](https://bit.ly/4bx8g3w?ref=scispace.com)

### Research Rabbit

[Research Rabbit](https://www.researchrabbit.ai/?ref=scispace.com) is a research discovery tool that helps you find new, connected papers using a visual graph. You can essentially create maps around metadata, which helps you not only explore similar papers but also connections between them.

### Iris AI

[Iris AI](https://iris.ai/?ref=scispace.com) is a specialized tool that understands the context of your research question, lets you apply smart filters, and finds relevant papers. Further, you can also extract summaries and other data from papers.

### ChatGPT

If you already don’t know about [ChatGPT](https://chat.openai.com/auth/login?ref=scispace.com), you must be living under a rock. ChatGPT is a chatbot that creates text based on a prompt using natural language processing (NLP). You can use it to write the first draft of your literature review, refine your writing, format it properly, write a research presentation, and many more things.

## **Things to keep in mind when using literature review AI tools**

While AI-powered tools can significantly streamline the literature review process, there are a few things you should keep in mind while employing them:

### Quality control

Always review the results generated by AI tools. AI is powerful but not infallible. Ensure that you do further analysis by yourself and determine that the selected research articles are indeed relevant to your research.

### Ethical considerations

Be aware of ethical concerns, such as plagiarism and AI writing. Use of AI is still frowned upon so make sure you do a thorough check for originality of your work, which is vital for maintaining academic integrity.

### Stay updated

The world of AI is ever-evolving. Stay updated on the latest advancements in AI tools for literature review to make the most of your research.

## In conclusion

[Artificial intelligence is a game-changer](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/02683962211048201?ref=scispace.com) for researchers, especially when it comes to literature reviews. It not only saves time but also enhances the quality and comprehensiveness of your work. With the right AI tool and a clear research question in hand, you can build an excellent literature review.

## How to Use AI for Literature Reviews: A Step-by-Step Workflow with Dr. Faheem and SciSpace

Systematic literature reviews (SLRs) are the foundation of rigorous research but let’s be honest, they’re often overwhelming.

In a recent [**SciSpace webinar**](https://lu.ma/scispace?ref=scispace.com), Dr. Faheem Ullah, drawing on his PhD experience and consulting work, shared how researchers can go from a vague ideas into structured, publication-ready review with the help of SciSpace, a powerful **AI tool for literature review.**

If you're unsure where to begin, how to structure your review, or how to make the most of **AI tools for research**, this walkthrough is for you.

## **Table of Contents**

- Step 1: Define the Right Research Question
- Step 2: Discover Relevant Topics with Topic Finder
- Step 3: Review Efficiently with Deep Review
- Step 4: Organise Insights in SciSpace Notebook
- Step 5: Draft Structured Paragraphs Using AI Writer
- Bonus Tip
- Final Thoughts + Webinar Replay + Discount Codes

* * *

## **Step 1: Define Your Research Scope with Clarity**

Dr. Faheem emphasised that every strong literature review starts with clarity, not just keywords. Before gathering papers, define what you’re exploring and what you are leaving out. Whether it's a scoping review or a full SLR, having a clear framework from the start prevents wasted efforts.

**How to define Research Scope using SciSpace:**

- Go to [**Find Topics**](https://scispace.com/concepts?ref=scispace.com) on SciSpace
- Type in your broad research theme (e.g., climate policy and agriculture)
- Review the AI-generated subtopics and citation-backed areas
- Select 2–3 sharp research questions and save these to your notebook

This step will give youa refined research scope backed by citation-rich subfields, and a shortlist of focused research questions you’ll revisit throughout the review.

## **Step 2: Create a Smart Workplace for Your Review**

Dr. Faheem stressed the importance of a living document that captures your findings, reflections, and citations. That’s where SciSpace’s [**Library**](https://scispace.com/library?ref=scispace.com) **and Notebooks** come in.

**How to build your research hub using SciSpace:**

- Open [**Library**](https://scispace.com/library?ref=scispace.com) and create a notebook in your SciSpace workspace
- Create a section for each research questions or subtopic
- Add summaries, notes, and quotes
- Use tags like ✅ (complete), 🔍 (needs follow-up), or ❗(important) to track your progress
- Use SciSpace's AI to clarify complex terms or simplify dense academic language

This step will give youa growing, well-organised digital hub that stores all your insights, thoughts, and citations ready to feed into your final literature review.

## **Step 3: Scan the Literature Smarter, Not Harder**

Literature reviews can feel like an endless scroll through PDFs. Dr. Faheem showed how to use SciSpace’s [**Deep Review**](https://scispace.com/search?ref=scispace.com), an advanced **literature review AI tool**, to surface relevant, citation-rich insights based on your research question.

**How to do literature review using SciSpace:**

- Head to [Literature Review](https://scispace.com/search?ref=scispace.com) tab
- Choose your review depth. Choose Standard Mode for a quick scan (~50 papers), High-Quality Mode for peer-reviewed, credible sources (~150 papers), Deep Mode for a comprehensive review (700+ papers)
- Enter your query, like 'What interventions have proven effective for improving adolescent mental health in low-income countries?'
- Review AI summarised insights, sorted by relevance, recency, and citation strength
- Click Save to Notebook on the most useful responses, along with links to full papers

This step will give you a curated, citation-backed evidence base for each research question, with zero manual downloading, or shifting through PDFs.

## **Step 4: Organise Your Evidence**

Once you’ve pulled in AI-curated insights from Deep Review, the next step is to separate the signal from the noise. Dr. Faheem stressed the importance of evidence mapping knowing what supports your argument, what contradicts it, and what’s just tangential.

**How to organise your research in SciSpace:**

- Go to the [**Library**](https://scispace.com/library?ref=scispace.com)& Notebooksto review all saved insights
- Group notes using research question or theme
- Highlight data points, or findings that directly support your question
- Use the [**Citation Generator**](https://scispace.com/citation-generator/apa-28t1c?ref=scispace.com) for quick referencing

This step will give you a clean, organised matrix of high-quality evidence already tagged, cited, and sorted for synthesis.

## **Step 5: Synthesise Across Studies with Chat with Folder**

Reading 100+ papers manually is exhausting and often inefficient. Dr. Faheem demonstrated how [**Chat with PDFs**](https://scispace.com/chat-pdf?ref=scispace.com) **,** SciSpace’s built-in **AI research assistant** streamlines this step by helping you spot patterns, contradictions, and consensus across all your saved PDFs at once.

**How to chat with PDFs in SciSpace:**

- Upload your reviewed papers to a Folder in SciSpace Library
- Select the files and click "chat with files"
- Ask questions like, 'What are the main factors influencing the research topic?'or Use follow-up prompts to go deeper, e.g., 'Which study designs reported the strongest outcomes?'
- Copy key summaries and citations back to your Notebook for final drafting

This step will give you acomparative, AI-generated insights across your entire paper set, summarised by theme, ready to inform your final synthesis or discussion section.

## **Bonus Tip: Turn Notes into Paragraphs with AI Writer**

Writing is often where researchers get stuck. Dr. Faheem recommended letting AI assist in early drafting. Once your notes are ready, use [**AI Writer**](https://scispace.com/ai-writer?ref=scispace.com), SciSpace’s **AI for academic research writing**, to go from bullets to citation-backed paragraphs in seconds.

**Here’s how:**

- Go to [**AI Writer**](https://scispace.com/ai-writer?ref=scispace.com) and select the 'Literature Review' template
- Paste bullet points or summaries from your Notebook
- Use Ask AI, And AI Suggestions to build your draft
- Use [**Paraphrase**](https://scispace.com/paraphraser?ref=scispace.com), Expand, or ImproveClarity to polish your output
- Copy final version to your document or save back to your Notebook

This step will give you crisp academic paragraphs that stay true to your research, minus the writing stress.

## **Final Thoughts**

Dr. Faheem’s workflow showed how **AI tools for research** can help you review faster, stay focused, and write with confidence. With the right tools, you can move from idea to execution without losing rigour.
